title: Brain Tissue Dissociation for Cell Sorting
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"45eg8","text":"  This is our standard brain tissue dissociation protocol for cell sorting (Human Cell Atlas).\n \n ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":76,"length":16}],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"89FF54B77C904ABBAF578E04937462CA","url":"https://www.humancellatlas.org/"}}}}

steps: Upon notification of an imminent donation, prepare a sample collection kit. Using a shipping box with an insulated container that can be directly used for return transportation of the tissue sample, include materials listed in the following steps. A critical component of this protocol requires proper handling of the tissue donation starting immediately at the time of autopsy. The overall sample viability depends significantly on minimizing the post-mortem interval (PMI), immediately transferring tissue into the appropriate cold medium supplemented with antibiotics/antimycotics, keeping the sample on wet ice throughout transportation, and maintaining sterile technique throughout the protocol.
Include materials for sterile preparation, including sterile gloves, drapes, and scalpels.

Include 8-10 sterile 50 mL conical tubes containing 30 mL shipping media in the insulated container with ice or cold packs. While any standard cell culture media can work, the best sample viability is obtained with Hibernate-A supplemented with antibiotic/antimycotic. undetermined, Shipping media
Include any further sample tubes for other analyses (e.g. fixatives, RNA-later).

If the autopsy will not be performed at the investigator’s site, include a document that clearly states how to collect the tumor sample. This includes details such as maintaining sterility, keeping sample tubes on wet ice, and including samples from the midbrain and medulla as tumor cells often invade these regions.

Maintain sterility during the autopsy. Consider the brain sterile inside the cranium, so observe sterile technique (including changing gloves) once the cranium is open. Avoiding contamination with skin and hair is critical.

Dissect the brain region of interest. With a sterile scalpel, cut small 1 cm chunks from the tumor and immediately transfer into cold shipping media (see NOTE in step 3) and put on wet ice. Place 10 mL of tissue into each tube, resulting in a final volume of tissue and media in each tube of 40 mL.
After sample collection, ship the samples overnight on wet ice in the insulated container. Do not ship the sample on dry ice, as freezing will kill the cells.

Prior to beginning sample preparation, sterilize tissue culture hoods along with razor blades, curved hemostats, and any other non-sterile tools under UV light for 1 hour. Perform all of the following steps under sterile conditions to prevent contamination of the cultures. 01:00:00, UV light
Prepare solutions as follows. [Sucrose Solution] To prepare 1.8 M sucrose solution, first, dissolve 308.07 g sucrose in 300 mL distilled water. undetermined, Sucrose undetermined, Distilled water

[Sucrose Solution] Add 50 mL 10X Hank’s Buffered Saline Solution (HBSS) without calcium or magnesium and bring total volume to 500 mL using distilled water. Store at 4°C. undetermined, 10X Hank’s Buffered Saline Solution (HBSS) Undetermined, Storage

[ Enzymatic Digestion Solution ] To prepare enzymatic digestion solution, first, take 50 mL HBSS with calcium and magnesium for every 10 mL of minced tissue and add 500 µL 5 mg/mL deoxyribonuclease I (final concentration 50 µg/mL), 500 µL 25 mg/mL Worthington STEMxyme I (final concentration 250 µg/mL), and 500 µL 1M HEPES buffer. undetermined, HBSS with calcium and magnesium for every 10 mL undetermined, 5 mg/mL deoxyribonuclease I undetermined, 25 mg/mL Worthington STEMxyme I undetermined, 1M HEPES buffer Different enzymatic digestions solution mixes can be used at this step. We have also used Liberase DH at a final concentration of 25 µg/mL.
[ Enzymatic Digestion Solution ] Prewarm digestion solution in a 37 °C water bath. Undetermined, Digestion solution
Pre-cool a laboratory centrifuge equipped with a swinging-bucket rotor to 4°C. Undetermined, Pre-cooling centrifuge
Transfer the tissue into a high-walled 100 x 20 mm cell culture dish.
Remove media leftover from shipping and replace with 10-15 mL cold culture media (e.g., Hibernate-A).
Using the curved hemostats to grasp a razor blade, mince the tissue finely while removing obvious blood vessels or meninges. The final tissue fragments should be smaller than 1 mm.
Transfer the tissue into a clean 50 mL conical tube.

Wash the cell culture dish with an additional 5 mL cold culture media and transfer to the conical tube. Repeat this wash step as necessary to transfer remaining tissue. undetermined, Cold culture media
Using a 10 mL serological pipet, triturate gently (4-5 times). Allow larger tissue fragments to settle to the bottom of the tube.
If there is more than 5 mL of tissue in the tube containing the minced tissue fragments, split the tissue into other new 50 mL conical tubes such that no tube has more than 5 mL tissue.
Centrifuge the conical tube(s) containing the remaining tissue fragments for 5 min (350 x g, 4°C). Undetermined, Centrifugation 00:05:00, Centrifugation
Remove the supernatant and add the prewarmed enzymatic digestion solution, such that there is 5 mL digestion solution for every 1 mL tissue. E.g. 25 mL digestion solution for 5 mL tissue.
Seal the conical tube lids with laboratory film and incubate the reaction on a rotator at 37°C for 30 min. Undetermined, Incubation 00:30:00, Incubation
After the incubation, to triturate the samples gently, first, using a 10 mL serological pipet, pipet the sample up and down 6-8 times. Avoid generating excessive air bubbles.
Add a 1000 µL pipet tip to the end of the pipet and triturate an additional 6-8 times.
Allow any remaining chunks to settle to the bottom of the tube.
Remove and filter the supernatant with the cells still suspended through a 100 µm filter into a new 50 mL conical tube labeled 'Enzymatic Dissociation' and store on ice.

If significant tissue fragments remain in the unfiltered fraction, add an additional 10 mL HBSS with calcium and magnesium to the fragments and repeat steps 25-28, combining the additional HBSS into the previously filtered supernatant in the 'Enzymatic Dissociation' tube. go to step #26
Centrifuge the 'Enzymatic Dissociation' tube for 5 min (350 x g, 4°C) and continue to sucrose gradient centrifugation (next step). Undetermined, Centrifugation 00:05:00, Centrifugation
If samples are still suspended in solution, centrifuge for 5 min (350 x g, 4°C) Undetermined, Centrifugation 00:05:00, Centrifugation
Remove the supernatant and resuspend tissue in 20 mL cold HBSS without calcium and magnesium. Bring volume up to 25 mL total with cold HBSS. undetermined, Cold HBSS without calcium and magnesium
Slowly add 25 mL 1.8 M sucrose solution and invert the tube to mix. undetermined, 1.8 M sucrose solution This results in a 0.9 M sucrose gradient.
Centrifuge with no brake for 10 min (800 x g, 4°C). Using a centrifugation brake will disrupt the gradient and reduce yield. Undetermined, Centrifugation 00:10:00, Centrifugation
Carefully aspirate myelin debris and as much sucrose solution as possible.
Wash the sample by adding 30 mL cold HBSS without calcium and magnesium and mixing gently. undetermined, Cold HBSS without calcium and magnesium
Centrifuge for 5 min (350 x g, 4°C). Undetermined, Centrifugation 00:05:00, Centrifugation
Remove the wash supernatant.
Add 5 mL ACK lysis buffer and gently resuspend the cell pellet, swirling the tube for 1 min at room temperature. undetermined, ACK lysis buffer 00:01:00, Swirling
Quench the lysis by adding 30 mL cold HBSS without calcium and magnesium. undetermined, Cold HBSS without calcium and magnesium
Centrifuge for 5 min (350 x g, 4°C). Undetermined, Centrifugation 00:05:00, Centrifugation
Resuspend samples in appropriate staining buffer for subsequent analysis, such as flow cytometry and fluorescence-activated cell sorting.
Filter the Sucrose and Enzymatic Digestion solutions through a sterile (0.22μm) filter
creator: Grant Lin
